source_paper.title: Improved tissue cryopreservation using inductive heating of magnetic nanoparticles

extraction_metadata.extraction_notes: Landmark nanowarming paper. Demonstrates that magnetic nanoparticle-based inductive heating can achieve uniform, rapid warming of vitrified tissues at rates sufficient to avoid devitrification. This addresses the critical warming rate bottleneck.

warming_rate_achieved.notes: Volumetric heating — every point in the sample heats simultaneously. Avoids thermal gradients that cause cracking.

biological_outcomes.outcome: Nanowarmed tissues showed significantly better viability and structural integrity than convectively warmed controls
biological_outcomes.viability_value: ~80% viability recovery in nanowarmed vs ~20% in convectively warmed

biological_outcomes.outcome: Successful vitrification and nanowarming with preserved mechanical properties

key_results.cracking: No cracking observed (major improvement over convective warming)
key_results.nanoparticle_removal: Nanoparticles can be washed out after warming

formulations_used.name: DP6 + mNPs (nanowarming)

warming_rate_achieved.notes: Sufficient to exceed DP6's CWR of 189°C/min in small samples, but marginal. Larger volumes may be insufficient.

formulations_used.notes: DP6 with nanowarming is attractive because DP6 is less toxic, but its high CWR (189°C/min) means nanowarming needs to achieve very high rates. For larger volumes, VS55 or M22 are safer choices.

technology_implications.why_nanowarming_matters: Warming, not cooling, is the dominant bottleneck in organ-scale vitrification. Convective warming creates thermal gradients that cause devitrification and cracking. Nanowarming provides volumetric, uniform heating that solves both problems simultaneously.
technology_implications.scale_up_potential: Demonstrated at 80mL. Active research to scale to organ volumes (hundreds of mL to liters).
technology_implications.current_limitations: Nanoparticle biocompatibility, FDA regulatory pathway, cost of nanoparticles, penetration into dense tissues
